does: This paper examines regulatory frameworks for advanced AI systems that pose potential risks to public safety. It analyzes the landscape of existing and proposed regulations across jurisdictions, identifies gaps in current governance structures, and proposes a comprehensive approach to managing risks from frontier AI capabilities. The work evaluates different regulatory strategies—including licensing, mandatory safety testing, transparency requirements, and oversight mechanisms—and assesses their trade-offs in terms of innovation, safety, and implementation feasibility. The paper synthesizes insights from risk management in other high-consequence domains like pharmaceuticals, aviation, and nuclear energy to develop evidence-based recommendations for AI governance.
connects: This work builds on growing literature examining AI governance and safety, extending discussions that emerged from earlier analyses of AI risks and regulation. It connects to prior research on AI safety concerns, capability forecasting, and systemic risks from advanced systems, while also drawing from established regulatory models in high-stakes industries. The paper responds to recent calls for proactive governance frameworks as AI systems become more powerful, building on theoretical work about AGI risks and practical policy analyses from organizations studying AI alignment and safety. It engages with ongoing debates about regulatory approaches versus industry self-governance, advancing beyond preliminary frameworks by grounding recommendations in comparative regulatory analysis.
enables: This work provides a foundation for policymakers designing AI-specific regulatory infrastructure and institutions. It enables more informed governance decisions by clarifying trade-offs between different regulatory approaches and identifying which mechanisms are most suited to managing specific categories of AI risks. The analysis supports development of international coordination frameworks for AI governance, pointing toward mechanisms for harmonizing regulations across jurisdictions. It also enables future research into implementation details of proposed regulatory approaches and their real-world effectiveness. By establishing clearer regulatory conceptual frameworks, the paper facilitates the transition from theoretical AI safety discussions to practical institutional design for managing frontier AI systems at scale.
